title: Trace a Page from File to Pixels
summary: Investigate a broken community notice page by predicting, arranging, inspecting, debugging, building, and explaining the browser pipeline.
objectives: Trace the hardware, operating-system, file, and browser responsibilities in a page load.
Distinguish saved HTML source from the DOM and rendered output.
Repair a document shell and verify the result with structural evidence.

steps.title: Predict the system
steps.instruction: Choose the explanation that accounts for the complete system behind the visible page.
steps.why: Web work becomes easier to debug when you can separate hardware, operating-system, application, and data responsibilities.

content.title: Incident brief
content.text: A volunteer double-clicks notice.html. A browser window opens and shows text. What had to cooperate?

hints: The browser cannot read storage or draw pixels without lower system layers.
Look for an answer that names data, the operating system, the browser, and hardware.
Choose the complete chain rather than a browser-only explanation.

options.text: The browser independently finds the file and draws the page without operating-system help.
Input hardware signals the operating system, which locates file data for the browser to parse and send through graphics hardware to the display.
The HTML file executes itself and tells the monitor which words to show.

steps.title: Build the load chain
steps.instruction: Put the events in causal order from the learner action to visible output.
steps.why: Ordering the mechanism exposes which layer to inspect when the expected output never appears.

content.text: Reuse the complete-system model. This time, construct the chain instead of recognizing it.

hints: Start with the physical action and end with emitted light.
The operating system reads storage before the browser can parse file data.
Order: input, file read, browser processing, display output.

options.text: Input device reports the open-file action
Operating system reads bytes from storage
Browser interprets the received document data
Graphics system and display produce visible pixels

steps.title: Inspect the evidence
steps.instruction: Inspect the project tree and identify the file that supplies the document structure.
steps.why: File roles and paths must be understood before a browser can reliably load linked resources.

stimulus.title: community-notice
stimulus.caption: A project folder captured before the page was opened.

hints: HTML represents document structure and content.
The stylesheet describes presentation, not the document tree.
Choose notice.html.

options.text: notice.html
styles.css
images/tools.jpg

steps.title: Source is not the DOM
steps.instruction: Read the field note, then confirm which claim the available evidence supports.
steps.why: A saved file and the browser-built representation are related evidence, but they are not the same object.

content.text: Source is the text saved in a file or returned by a server. The browser parses that text and constructs an in-memory document object model, or DOM. Error recovery, scripts, and browser rules can make the DOM differ from the exact source text.

content.title: Evidence rule
content.text: Inspect source to learn what arrived. Inspect the DOM to learn what the browser currently represents.

hints: The browser creates the DOM after receiving source.
Error recovery can change the tree without changing the saved file.
Choose the claim that treats source and DOM as related but distinct evidence.

options.text: Source and DOM are always identical character for character.
Source is input text; the DOM is the browser's parsed, current document representation.
The DOM is another name for the page stylesheet.

steps.title: Predict browser recovery
steps.instruction: Predict what the browser does when it receives a paragraph without an explicit body element.
steps.why: Browsers recover from many source errors, so visible output alone cannot prove that source is complete or intentional.

content.caption: Incomplete source received by a browser

hints: HTML parsers have defined error-recovery behavior.
The browser can construct missing structural nodes in the DOM.
It builds html, head, and body nodes and places the paragraph in body.

options.text: It refuses to create a document until body is typed.
It constructs required document nodes and places the paragraph into the DOM body.
It converts the paragraph into a CSS rule.

steps.title: Trace source to pixels
steps.instruction: Arrange the simplified browser pipeline in causal order.
steps.why: The pipeline connects saved code to inspectable structures and final visual output.

content.text: Zoom in on the browser portion of the full system chain you built earlier.

hints: A browser must receive source before parsing it.
The document tree exists before layout and paint use it.
Order: receive source, parse, build DOM, layout and paint.

options.text: Receive document source
Tokenize and parse HTML
Construct the document tree
Compute layout and paint pixels

steps.title: Read the browser-built tree
steps.instruction: Inspect the DOM snapshot and identify evidence of browser-created structure.
steps.why: DOM inspection verifies what the browser represents after parsing rather than what source merely appears to say.

stimulus.title: Elements panel snapshot
stimulus.caption: The browser representation after parsing the incomplete paragraph source.

hints: Compare the snapshot with the incomplete source from two steps ago.
The source did not explicitly contain html, head, or body tags.
The created head and body nodes prove parser recovery.

options.text: The paragraph has black text.
The DOM contains html, head, and body nodes absent from the source.
The filename ends in .html.

steps.title: Repair the document shell
steps.instruction: Add an English language declaration and a useful Repair clinic page title to the starter HTML.
steps.why: A complete shell gives browsers and assistive technology the language and document identity missing from visible-only checks.

content.title: Two hidden defects
content.text: The page renders, but its root language and browser-tab title are missing. Repair source—not the temporary DOM snapshot.

stimulus.title: Conformance report
stimulus.caption: Two structural defects found before the page is published.

hints: The language belongs on the root html start tag; the title belongs inside head.
Use lang="en" and a title element with meaningful text.
Write <html lang="en"> and <title>Repair clinic</title>.

steps.title: Add meaningful structure
steps.instruction: Wrap the visible clinic notice in a main element and give the page an h1 named Community repair clinic.
steps.why: The same page now grows from valid machine context into a meaningful content hierarchy without discarding earlier repairs.

content.text: Keep both repairs from the previous step. Add one bounded layer of semantic structure around the existing notice.

hints: Do not replace the repaired document shell.
Place main inside body, then place h1 and the existing paragraph inside main.

Use

Community repair clinic

...

.

steps.title: Explain with evidence
steps.instruction: Explain how saved source, the DOM, and rendered pixels differ, and name which evidence you would inspect for each.
steps.why: Explaining the model from memory exposes gaps that recognition and copying can hide.

content.title: Transfer brief
content.text: A teammate says, ‘It looks right, so the source must be right.’ Respond using the evidence model you practiced.

hints: Define each artifact before explaining how you inspect it.
Include the words source, DOM, and pixels, then connect each to evidence.
Source is saved input, DOM is the parsed tree, and pixels are painted output; inspect all three separately.

checks.description: The explanation includes the complete computer and browser chain.
checks.failureMessage: Account for the system layers below and around the browser.

checks.description: The page-load events follow a valid causal order.
checks.failureMessage: Trace the learner action before storage, browser work, and display output.

checks.description: The HTML file is identified as the document-structure source.
checks.failureMessage: Separate document structure from styling and image resources.

checks.description: The claim distinguishes source input from the browser-built DOM.
checks.failureMessage: Treat source and DOM as related but distinct evidence.

checks.description: The prediction accounts for browser parser error recovery.
checks.failureMessage: Consider the required nodes a browser constructs during parsing.

checks.description: The browser processing stages follow a valid simplified order.
checks.failureMessage: Put received input and parsing before structures and painted output.

checks.description: The selected evidence identifies browser-created DOM nodes.
checks.failureMessage: Compare the exact source with the later tree snapshot.

checks.description: The root html element declares English content.
checks.failureMessage: Add the expected language attribute to the root element.

checks.description: The document title names the repair clinic.
checks.failureMessage: Add a useful title inside the document head.

checks.description: A main region contains the primary notice content.
checks.failureMessage: Wrap the page's unique primary content in main.

checks.description: The page heading names the community repair clinic.
checks.failureMessage: Add the requested h1 inside the main region.

checks.description: The explanation distinguishes source, DOM, and pixel evidence.
checks.failureMessage: Name all three artifacts and explain how their evidence differs.
